lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly provide outsourced refrigerated and frozen storage, handling, inventory control, blast freezing, case picking, inspection, and related cold-chain warehouse services to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive food-producer or retailer warehouses, passive real-estate shells, refrigerated transportation without warehousing, general dry storage, household cold lockers, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without recurring or repeat external-customer service revenue.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Food producers, processors, distributors, retailers, and selected life-sciences customers buy recurring temperature-controlled space plus transactional handling and value-added services under warehouse agreements, rate letters, tariffs, and occasionally dedicated leases, often with minimum storage commitments and price-adjustment clauses.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the broader NAICS 493100 occupation mix, not six-digit refrigerated warehousing or task-level exposure. Transportation and material-moving jobs are 75.38% of employment and office support is 10.13%; BLS identifies AGVs, robotics, collaborative automation, and AS/RS as reducing core warehouse staffing. Cold-specific operator disclosures and industry evidence show labor is the largest operating-cost component and that pallet storage, movement, scheduling, and control can be automated. Applying higher partial exposure to standardized pallet movement and clerical/planning tasks may overstate older irregular facilities but understate the economic incentive to remove people from freezer environments.
a.rationale: Cold warehouses have substantial repetitive pallet movement, picking, inventory, appointment, labor-planning, and documentation work. AI can support decisions and controls while robotics handles structured movement, but loading variability, maintenance, sanitation, food safety, inspections, exceptions, and already-automated tasks remain outside the opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted activity study across 20 independent 493120 operators, recording paid hours by workflow and current WMS, WES, AS/RS, pallet shuttle, AMR, vision, and AI use, would materially move exposure.
caveats: The occupational source combines all warehousing and storage industries and employer sizes.
Lineage is a global scale leader whose automation footprint and workflow standardization differ from LMM firms.
The frozen compensation-to-output input is sector-specific but does not reveal the wage share of each task.

rho.bridge: Cold-chain sources document feasible tools and deployments rather than the five-year share of exposed opportunity implemented by US LMM firms. MHI describes cold-capable AS/RS, pallet shuttles, AMRs, conveyors, and WMS/WES integration; a five-site provider deployed ML labor forecasting from two years of WMS data; Lineage reports only 83 fully or semi-automated warehouses within a 501-warehouse global portfolio. I discount implementation for LMM capital, food-safety validation, integration, facility-age, and refrigeration constraints. Large-provider evidence likely overstates resources but may understate the accessibility of software-only use cases.
rho.rationale: Predictive staffing, appointment scheduling, exception triage, reporting, billing, and inventory analytics can be deployed within five years, while high-bay automation and facility redesign require much more capital and downtime. Cold-environment equipment, customer integrations, and safety/compliance make full conversion slower.
rho.change_evidence: A vendor-neutral panel of LMM cold-storage projects showing capital cost, implementation duration, commissioning failures, food-safety validation, throughput, and realized labor hours per pallet before and after deployment would materially change rho.
caveats: The CSW case study is vendor-authored and reports capabilities rather than a quantified labor reduction.
MHI is an industry association for material-handling suppliers and users, not a representative adoption survey.
Automation can be economically attractive in new high-throughput buildings but infeasible in smaller brownfield sites.

e.rationale: Firms classified in 493120 generally operate temperature-controlled warehousing as their primary business, so most LMM firms should sell recurring external service. The range removes captive or misclassified facilities, passive property entities, leased facilities where the customer operates the warehouse, and businesses whose customer concentration, facility condition, or compliance status prevents a transferable recurring-service operation.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 100 LMM-band 493120 firms from Census-linked or commercial records, coded for public-for-hire status, external-customer revenue, contracts, ownership independence, customer concentration, food-safety standing, and transferable operations, would materially move eligibility.
caveats: USDA counts facilities rather than firms and includes private and semiprivate warehouses outside the default lens.
The frozen n input is margin-bridged and may misclassify capital-intensive firms whose normalized EBITDA differs from the assumed transportation margin.
Dedicated facilities may have recurring revenue but depend on a single customer and specific real estate.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, but this is cross-industry intent, not completed LMM refrigerated-warehouse control transfers. Lineage disclosures confirm repeated completed acquisitions of US cold-storage operating assets in 2023-25, including Bellingham, ColdPoint, Burris, and NOVA, establishing strategic buyer demand without supplying an industry denominator. I discount stated intent for failed, delayed, non-control, and closure outcomes, while allowing a higher center than many small-business sectors because specialized networks are actively consolidating.
s5.rationale: Aging owners and demonstrated strategic acquisition appetite support meaningful five-year transfer probability. Capital intensity, environmental diligence, food-safety records, customer concentration, and facility obsolescence still prevent many planned exits from becoming qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed and failed cold-storage transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and specialist brokers, matched to 493120 EBITDA and ownership type, would materially move the probability.
caveats: Gallup includes employer firms of all industries and sizes and measures plans rather than completions.
Lineage acquisitions are examples from one dominant buyer and do not establish an annual transfer rate.
The estimate excludes real-estate-only purchases, captive asset transfers without an external-service business, minority recapitalizations, and closures.

q.bridge: Lineage's 2025 filing describes global temperature-controlled contracts rather than LMM US outcomes: warehouse agreements can have one-to-five-year terms, transactional service pricing, minimum storage guarantees, and cost/profile price adjustments; shorter rate letters and tariffs can reprice within a year. These mechanics imply savings can be retained under fixed transactional prices before repricing, but customer-specific cost-to-serve reviews and frequent tariff updates share gains. Scale and network power may cause Lineage to overstate LMM retention, while specialized compliance and switching costs may offset that bias.
q.rationale: Implemented labor savings should improve contribution on per-pallet, per-move, and minimum-commitment revenue during a contract term. Over five years, renewals, customer benchmarking, negotiated productivity commitments, short-term rate letters, and competition return a substantial share to customers, leaving meaningful but incomplete retention.
q.change_evidence: A review of 50 LMM refrigerated-warehouse contracts and renewals, coding term, minimum commitments, transactional versus cost-plus pricing, productivity or gain-share clauses, open-book rights, escalators, and actual rebid changes, would materially move q.
caveats: The source is a global public operator substantially larger than the target firms.
Dedicated build-to-suit, shared public warehouse, rate-letter, tariff, and lease economics differ materially.
Energy and refrigeration costs can obscure labor savings in customer pricing and operator margins.

d5.bridge: USDA measures refrigerated warehouse capacity, not constant-quality outsourced service quantity: total US gross capacity rose from 3.70 billion cubic feet in 2023 to 3.99 billion in 2025, while public-for-hire capacity fell from 2.51 billion to 2.46 billion and private/semiprivate capacity rose. A 2026 vendor survey of 1,000 North American food-and-beverage decision-makers found 72% reporting rising refrigerated/frozen-food demand. I interpret these as moderate underlying growth with an outsourcing-share warning, rather than extrapolating the two-year construction surge. Capacity can overstate demand when vacancy rises; customer sentiment can overstate realized volumes.
d5.rationale: Frozen and refrigerated foods, food safety, supply-chain resilience, and product integrity sustain the service basket, but inventory cycles, new speculative capacity, food-volume weakness, and customer insourcing temper growth. The base assumes modest five-year quantity expansion rather than repeating recent capacity additions.
d5.change_evidence: A US five-year panel of public refrigerated warehouse occupied pallet-days, throughput pallets, and value-added service units from GCCA members, adjusted for acquisitions, new capacity, price, and service mix, would materially narrow the range.
caveats: Capacity is supply, not utilization or constant-quality demand.
USDA's public/private classification is facility-based and the survey vintages are only two years apart.
The customer-demand survey covers the United States, Canada, and Mexico and was sponsored by the largest provider.
Public capacity contraction signals that total cold-chain expansion may not accrue proportionally to outsourced operators.

o.rationale: Software cannot itself refrigerate, hold, inspect, move, and assume responsibility for perishable goods. Most future outsourced quantity should still need an accountable warehouse operator for temperature control, food-safety compliance, custody, equipment, exceptions, and customer service. The haircut recognizes customer insourcing, vertically integrated facilities, and some network consolidation; USDA's decline in public capacity share shows that this is a real channel, while long operator relationships and minimum commitments indicate persistence.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level contract-loss data from LMM cold-storage operators, separating insourcing, software-only substitution, captive network buildout, consolidation, and competitor switching, plus interviews with 30 food producers and retailers, would materially change o.
caveats: No source directly measures the future share of outsourced cold-storage quantity requiring a lens operator.
Public capacity fell while private and semiprivate capacity expanded between USDA surveys, but capacity does not reveal occupied volume.
Automation changes staffing intensity more readily than it removes operator accountability.

narrative.executive_view: Refrigerated warehouses have structured, labor-intensive movement and information flows, strong incentives to remove people from harsh environments, and service demand anchored in physical product integrity. The opportunity is constrained by high capital needs, food-safety requirements, facility specificity, and evidence that recent capacity growth has favored private as well as public warehouses.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can forecast per-load labor, schedule appointments and shifts, optimize slotting and energy use, reconcile inventory, triage exceptions, and automate documentation and billing. Paired with pallet shuttles, AS/RS, AMRs, conveyors, and WES controls, it can reduce movement and picking labor, while humans remain essential for loading variability, sanitation, maintenance, compliance, and recovery from faults.
narrative.value_capture: Minimum storage guarantees and fixed transactional service rates can preserve productivity savings during contract terms. Short rate letters, annual tariffs, cost-to-serve reviews, renewal bargaining, and sophisticated food-company customers make retention incomplete over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: Properly classified independent cold stores generally provide recurring outsourced service, but the LMM pool is small and transferability depends on real estate, refrigeration assets, environmental condition, certifications, and customer concentration. Multiple completed acquisitions show strategic demand, but no denominator supports treating deal examples as an observed transfer rate.
narrative.demand_durability: Refrigerated product must remain within a controlled physical chain, and customer survey evidence points to rising frozen and refrigerated food demand. USDA capacity grew overall from 2023 to 2025, but public capacity declined as private capacity rose, so outsourced service growth should be underwritten below total cold-chain capacity growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: A bad outcome combines expensive automation with an unsuitable legacy building, poor WMS data, commissioning downtime, food-safety failures, and a customer contract that reprices the benefit away. Demand can also disappoint through inventory destocking, speculative overbuilding, customer insourcing, consolidation, or loss of a concentrated account.
narrative.principal_driver: Standardized pallet workflows and harsh cold environments create strong labor-avoidance incentives while physical custody and temperature control remain indispensable.
narrative.principal_weakness: Capital-intensive implementation and a small, consolidating firm pool are compounded by uncertainty over whether future capacity growth stays outsourced or shifts to captive networks.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 493100 Warehousing and Storage
sources.supports: Broader warehousing occupation mix and wages: transportation/material-moving 75.38% of employment, office support 10.13%, and management 2.93%, with detailed core warehouse roles.

sources.title: Factors affecting occupational utilization, 2024-34
sources.supports: BLS identifies AGVs, robotics, collaborative automation, and automated storage/retrieval as reducing staffing shares in core NAICS 493000 material-moving, packing, and stocking occupations.

sources.title: Automation for the Cold Chain
sources.supports: Cold-specific labor difficulty and applicable automation including AS/RS, pallet shuttles, AMRs, conveyors, WCS, WES, and WMS integration, alongside temperature-zone equipment constraints.

sources.title: An Effective, AI-driven Approach to Predictive Labor Resourcing: CSW Success Story
sources.supports: A five-location third-party cold-storage provider used two years of WMS shipment and activity data to deploy ML-based hourly labor and throughput predictions.

sources.title: Lineage, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Cold-storage contract types, one-to-five-year typical warehouse agreements, transactional pricing, minimum guarantees, repricing mechanisms, labor as largest operating-cost component, 83 fully/semi-automated warehouses among 501, and long customer relationships.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 owner survey finding that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years.

sources.title: USDA NASS Capacity of Refrigerated Warehouses 2023 Summary
sources.supports: In 2023, US gross refrigerated capacity was 3.70 billion cubic feet; public warehouses held 2.51 billion cubic feet or 68% of total capacity; 492 of 900 facilities were public.

sources.title: USDA NASS Capacity of Refrigerated Warehouses 2025 Summary
sources.supports: In 2025, US gross refrigerated capacity was 3.99 billion cubic feet; public warehouses held 2.46 billion cubic feet or 62% of total capacity; 476 of 931 facilities were public.

sources.title: Lineage 2025 business combinations and asset acquisitions disclosure
sources.supports: Completed acquisitions of Bellingham Cold Storage in 2025, ColdPoint Logistics in 2024, and Burris and NOVA cold-storage operations in 2023, plus the 2025 Tyson warehouse asset acquisition.

sources.title: Lineage 2026 Cold Chain Insights Survey
sources.supports: Survey of 1,000 food-and-beverage supply-chain decision-makers across the US, Canada, and Mexico: 72% reported rising refrigerated/frozen-food demand and respondents prioritized AI, data, automation, and flexible 3PL capacity.
